name: Antigone
description: Antigone, daughter of Oedipus and Jocasta, is a central figure in Greek tragedy, renowned for her unwavering moral conviction and defiance of unjust authority. Born into a family cursed by fate, she faced immense hardship, including her father's tragic downfall and her brothers' fratricidal conflict. When Creon, the king of Thebes, forbade the burial of her brother Polynices, branding him a traitor, Antigone chose to uphold divine law and familial duty over human decree. She bravely defied Creon's edict, performing the burial rites for her brother, knowing full well the consequences. Her act of civil disobedience, driven by a deep sense of justice and respect for the dead, led to her imprisonment and eventual death. Antigone's story is a powerful exploration of the conflict between individual conscience and state power, the importance of moral courage, and the enduring strength of familial bonds. She stands as a symbol of resistance against tyranny and a testament to the power of individual action in the face of injustice. Her tragic fate serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of hubris and the importance of upholding moral principles, even in the face of death.
subtitle: Defiance, Duty, and Tragic Fate.
achievements: Upheld divine law and familial duty

weapons.name: Moral conviction
weapons.description: Her unshakeable belief in divine law and familial duty, which guided her actions.

weapons.name: Words
weapons.description: Her ability to articulate her principles and challenge Creon's authority.

weapons.name: Determination
weapons.description: Her unwavering resolve to honor her brother and defy unjust laws.

keyMyths.title: The Burial of Polynices
keyMyths.description: Creon forbids the burial of Polynices, declaring him a traitor. Antigone, defying the king's edict, secretly buries her brother, prioritizing divine law and familial duty.

keyMyths.title: Antigone's Imprisonment
keyMyths.description: After being caught burying Polynices, Antigone is condemned to death by Creon. Despite pleas from Haemon, Creon remains resolute in his judgment, leading to devastating consequences.

keyMyths.title: Antigone's Death
keyMyths.description: Antigone takes her own life in her tomb, choosing death over submission to Creon's unjust rule. Her death triggers a series of tragic events, including the suicides of Haemon and Eurydice.

sources.author: Sophocles
sources.description: A classic Greek tragedy detailing Antigone's defiance of Creon.
sources.text: A classic Greek tragedy detailing Antigone's defiance of Creon.

sources.author: Euripides
sources.description: References to the Oedipus cycle and the conflict between Polynices and Eteocles.
sources.text: References to the Oedipus cycle and the conflict between Polynices and Eteocles.

associations.name: Civil Disobedience
associations.significance: Represents the moral obligation to resist unjust laws.

associations.name: Filial Piety
associations.significance: Highlights the importance of family loyalty and duty.

associations.name: Tragedy
associations.significance: Embodies the tragic consequences of fate and human choices.

associations.name: Moral Courage
associations.significance: Represents bravery in upholding ethical principles.

associations.name: Sisterhood
associations.significance: Emphasizes the bonds and responsibilities between sisters, particularly in adversity.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both demonstrate unwavering loyalty and moral strength in the face of adversity, upholding dharma/divine law over personal safety.
Shares a similar spirit of defiance against unjust authority.
Esther risked her life to appeal to the king, defying protocol to save her people, similar to Antigone's stand against Creon.

worshipPractices.name: Funerary Rites
worshipPractices.description: While Antigone herself wasn't directly worshipped, her actions highlight the importance of proper burial rites in ancient Greek culture, which were seen as essential for the soul's journey to the afterlife. Families performed rituals to honor the dead, ensuring their peaceful transition.

cultural.modernLegacy: Antigone's story continues to resonate in modern literature, theater, and political discourse, serving as a symbol of resistance against unjust laws and the importance of individual conscience. Her character inspires discussions on civil disobedience, human rights, and the conflict between personal morality and state authority.

extendedContent.title: Early Life
extendedContent.content: Antigone's early life was marred by the tragic fate of her father, Oedipus, who unknowingly killed his father and married his mother, Jocasta. Born from this incestuous union, Antigone and her siblings, Ismene, Polynices, and Eteocles, were burdened by the family's curse. Despite the shame and hardship, Antigone remained devoted to her father, accompanying him in exile after he blinded himself upon discovering the truth. This early experience instilled in her a deep sense of duty and a strong moral compass, shaping her later actions. The weight of her family's history and the societal stigma she faced likely contributed to her unwavering commitment to justice and her willingness to defy authority, setting the stage for her tragic destiny.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests
extendedContent.content: Antigone's defining act of defiance came after the death of her brothers, Eteocles and Polynices, who killed each other in a battle for the throne of Thebes. Creon, the new king, declared Eteocles a hero and ordered him to be buried with honors, while Polynices, deemed a traitor, was forbidden burial, his body left to rot as a warning. Antigone, driven by her sense of familial duty and divine law, defied Creon's edict and secretly buried her brother, ensuring his soul could find peace. This act of civil disobedience, knowing it would lead to her own death, is her greatest 'quest', showcasing her unwavering commitment to her principles and her willingness to sacrifice herself for what she believed was right. It is not a physical quest, but a moral one.

extendedContent.title: Legacy
extendedContent.content: Antigone's legacy endures as a powerful symbol of moral courage and resistance against unjust authority. Her story has been reinterpreted and adapted throughout history, inspiring countless works of art, literature, and political thought. She represents the individual's right to conscience and the importance of challenging oppressive regimes. Her defiance of Creon's decree continues to resonate in modern debates about civil disobedience, human rights, and the limits of state power. Antigone's unwavering commitment to her principles, even in the face of death, serves as a timeless reminder of the importance of standing up for what is right, regardless of the consequences. Her character is a testament to the enduring power of human morality and the struggle for justice.

shortDescription: Daughter of Oedipus, famed for defying King Creon's unjust decree, burying her brother Polynices against his orders, showcasing unwavering moral conviction.
